draft.introduction: Chapter 4 of the Tao Te Ching describes the Tao as an empty vessel that is inexhaustible in its use. It establishes the paradox that emptiness is the source of all fullness.

translation.text: The Tao is like an empty vessel that yet may be drawn from without ever needing to be filled. It is bottomless—the very ancestor of all things. It blunts the sharp, unties the tangled, softens the glaring, and settles the dust. It is deep and still, and appears to have existed before the Lord.

translator_notes.note: The phrase "appears to have existed before the Lord" (xiang di zhi xian) is one of the most striking claims in the text. It places the Tao prior to any personalized deity, establishing a cosmology in which the impersonal principle precedes the personal god—a sharp contrast with the Abrahamic traditions.
